title: The Richest Man in Babylon
subtitle: George Clason — timeless principles of accumulation in parable form
description: A checklist based on the legendary book 'The Richest Man in Babylon' by George Clason, first published in 1926 and still relevant today. In the form of engaging parables about ancient Babylon, the author lays out fundamental laws for handling money: from the principle of 'pay yourself first' to the rules of investing and protecting capital. These truths, tested for millennia, work in any era and for any income level. The book teaches not complex financial instruments, but basic financial literacy and discipline — precisely what most people lack.

items.title: The Seven Cures for a Lean Purse: First Law — Pay Yourself First
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: Arkad, the richest man in Babylon, shares the secret of his wealth with childhood friends. The first and most important law: 'A part of all you earn is yours to keep, and you must keep it.' This is the fundamental principle of 'pay yourself first.' Arkad recommends saving at least one-tenth (10%) of any income, no matter how small. The psychological trap most people fall into: they pay everyone else first (rent, stores, entertainment) and keep what's left. But 'what's left' is usually zero. Change the order: as soon as you receive income, immediately set aside 10% in an inaccessible place. This is not about 'saving on everything,' it's about 'paying your future self.' Interestingly, according to Arkad, people who started saving 10% stopped noticing that they lived on 90% after a few months — their lifestyle adapted. This is the first step from poverty to wealth.
items.tip: Set up an automatic transfer of 10% from each incoming payment to a separate savings account on payday. Don't wait for 'whatever's left.' This is the first and most important payment — to your future self.

items.title: Second Law: Control Your Expenditures
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: Arkad's second law: 'Control your expenditures so you don't spend more than necessary on your true desires.' The paradox he observes: people often confuse 'necessary expenses' with 'desires.' What we call 'necessary expenses' usually grows with income. Salary increased — a more expensive phone, eating out instead of the canteen, branded clothes become 'necessary.' Arkad teaches: 'What each of us calls our 'necessary expenses' will always grow to equal our income unless we protest.' The solution is to track expenses and create a budget. Not to cut everything down to the bone, but to consciously choose what to spend on. A budget is not a prison, but a tool for freedom. It helps direct money toward what truly brings joy and value, and cut out mindless spending that 'eats' the wallet unnoticed.
items.tip: For 30 days, record absolutely all your expenses, down to the penny. Then analyze: which expenses were truly necessary and brought benefit/joy, and which were impulsive and forgotten after a day? Reduce the second category without touching the first.

items.title: Third Law: Make Your Gold Multiply
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: The third law: 'Make your gold multiply.' Savings are not the goal, but the means. Money just lying 'under the mattress' loses purchasing power due to inflation. Arkad teaches: each saved shekel must 'work' and 'breed' new shekels. In modern terms, this means investing. But Clason, through Arkad, warns: investments must be wise, not speculative. The investor's main quality is not greed, but prudence. Money must work for you, generating passive income that can eventually exceed your active income. That is the path to financial freedom. Arkad advises investing only in what you understand and consulting those who have already succeeded in that field. 'Advice is the only commodity that is cheap, but bad advice can cost a fortune.'
items.tip: Study the basic investment tools available to you: bank deposits with capitalization, bonds, index funds (ETFs). Start small, but start. Let your savings start earning even 1% extra — that's money already working.

items.title: Fourth Law: Guard Your Capital from Loss
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: Arkad's fourth law: 'Guard your treasures from loss.' This is a rule many ignore in the chase for high returns. The investor's first task is not to earn, but not to lose. Arkad tells the story of a brickmaker who, having saved some money, invested it in a jewel on a passerby's advice and lost everything. The moral: 'Small profit in safety is better than big risk with total loss.' Before investing money, ensure you understand what you're investing in and that your capital is protected. Don't invest in 'guaranteed get-rich-quick schemes.' Don't entrust your savings to those without a proven reputation. Consult experts who have themselves succeeded in the area you want to invest in. And most importantly — don't put all your eggs in one basket. Diversification is an ancient principle that Clason presents through a metaphor: 'Do not put all your gold on one ship, for ships sink.'
items.tip: Before any investment, ask yourself three questions: 1) Do I fully understand how this works? 2) Am I willing to lose this money completely without catastrophic consequences for my life? 3) Have I consulted someone who has already successfully invested in this?

items.title: Fifth Law: Make Your Home a Profitable Investment
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: The fifth law: 'Make your dwelling a profitable investment.' In the context of ancient Babylon, Arkad advised owning your own home to avoid paying rent, and using land for growing food. In a modern context, this law can be interpreted more broadly: strive to turn liabilities into assets. Own housing, if bought wisely, appreciates over time and frees you from endless rent payments. But Clason warns: the home should be affordable. Don't take on an unaffordable mortgage that will make you a 'slave to credit.' The home should bring benefit and savings, not be a source of constant stress. Furthermore, this law speaks to the importance of owning assets that provide everyday usefulness and can simultaneously be investments.
items.tip: Analyze your monthly rent payments. Calculate how much you will pay over 10 years. Compare with potential mortgage payments for similar property. Consider property appreciation and inflation. Possibly, owning is more profitable than renting in the long run.

items.title: Sixth Law: Provide Income in Old Age
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: The sixth law: 'Provide income for your future.' Arkad reminds us of the inevitability of old age and the need to prepare for the time when you can no longer or no longer wish to work with the same intensity. 'No man can afford to be defenseless against old age and decrepitude.' In ancient times, this meant accumulating land, livestock, jewelry. In the modern world — pension savings, an investment portfolio, rental property, or a business that runs without your involvement. The key idea: start thinking about this when young, when you have time and energy. Compound interest works best over long time horizons. Saving small amounts from age 25 yields much more by age 60 than saving large sums from age 50. Time is the investor's main ally.
items.tip: Open an individual investment account (IIS) or another pension account and start contributing at least a small amount monthly. Don't wait for the 'right moment' or 'when I earn more.' Start now, even with 1000 rubles.

items.title: Seventh Law: Enhance Your Ability to Earn
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 2
items.text: The seventh and final law of Arkad: 'Enhance your skills to increase your income.' This is perhaps the most important law because it addresses the root of wealth — your ability to create value. Arkad says: 'The more wisdom we acquire, the more we can earn.' Investing in yourself — in education, skills, health, connections — has the highest potential return, unavailable in any financial market. Skill improvement can increase your income by 20-50-100%, and this new income level stays with you forever. Clason emphasizes: 'The desire to become wiser in your profession and in handling money will be rewarded.' Read books, take courses, seek mentors, take on more complex tasks. Your main asset is yourself, and its depreciation is not foreseen, only growth.
items.tip: Identify one skill that, if upgraded, could increase your income in the next 6-12 months. It could be learning a new language, mastering software, getting a certificate. Invest time, and if needed, money, into this skill. Track how this increases your value in the market.

items.title: The Five Laws of Gold: Rules for Handling Capital
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 5
items.text: In a separate parable, Clason outlines the 'Five Laws of Gold,' which complement the seven cures. 1) Gold comes to those who save at least a tenth of their earnings. 2) Gold works and multiplies for those who invest it wisely. 3) Gold remains with those who consult wise people. 4) Gold slips away from those who invest it in ventures they don't understand. 5) Gold vanishes from those who chase fantastical profits or believe in swindlers. These laws are the quintessence of financial wisdom. Note the recurring motif: ignorance and greed are capital's main enemies. Modest but stable returns from understandable instruments are preferable to risky adventures. Seek advice from those who have already achieved what you aspire to, and who are willing to share their experience selflessly.
items.tip: Write down the Five Laws of Gold and post them in a visible place. Before any financial transaction, mentally check it against these laws. Especially laws 3, 4 and 5 — they protect against the most common mistakes.

items.title: The Parable of the Debtor: How to Get Out of the Debt Pit
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 8
items.text: Clason tells the story of the merchant Dabasir, who, fleeing debt and becoming a slave, managed to return, pay everything back to the last penny, and become wealthy. His plan for getting out of debt consists of three steps: 1) Acknowledge the debts and make a full list. Stop hiding and ignoring the problem. 2) Negotiate with creditors on an affordable repayment plan. Honestly explain the situation and propose to pay 20% of your income toward debts (proportionally to each creditor). 3) Live on 70% of your income: 10% save for yourself (first law), 20% repay debts, 70% live on. Dabasir emphasizes the importance of honesty and discipline. Creditors, seeing his sincere intention and regular, albeit small, payments, respected him, and he eventually restored his reputation. The moral: 'Where there is determination, there is a way.' Getting out of debt starts with a decision and a plan, not the amount.
items.tip: If you have debts, make a full list with amounts and interest rates. Calculate how much you can allocate monthly to repay them. Contact creditors and propose a plan. Remember: ignoring only worsens the problem.

items.title: The Goddess of Luck: Chance Favors the Prepared
items.source: George Clason — The Richest Man in Babylon, Chapter 7
items.text: In the parable of the goddess of luck, Clason reflects on the role of chance in wealth. Many blame their poverty on 'bad luck' and others' wealth on 'luck.' But Arkad argues: 'Luck favors those who are prepared to receive it.' He tells of the merchant who always carried a purse of gold in order not to miss a profitable deal, and the farmer who checked his fields daily and in time noticed a gold vein. 'Luck' is the intersection of preparation and opportunity. If you lack knowledge, have no savings, don't monitor opportunities, then even when 'luck' knocks, you won't be able to let it in. 'People tend to act when driven by need itself. But there is wisdom in acting when opportunity merely appears.' Create a 'purse for luck' — keep a reserve fund for unexpected profitable opportunities. Be prepared.
items.tip: Create an 'opportunity fund' — a separate small account intended specifically for unexpected profitable deals (discounts on needed items, stocks, courses, trips). When 'luck' knocks, you'll have something to greet it with.

quiz.q: What portion of income does Arkad, the richest man in Babylon, advise saving first?
Which of the 'Five Laws of Gold' states that gold slips away from those who invest it in ventures they don't understand?
What was the name of the merchant who fled his debts, became a slave, but returned and paid it all back?
According to the book, what is a person's main asset that needs to be developed?
a: One's own skills and ability to earn

quiz.q: How does Clason explain 'luck' in financial matters?
a: It is a random event independent of the person
It is the intersection of preparation and opportunity
It is a gift from the gods to the chosen
